questionText: You are replacing a condenser fan motor on an RTU. The unit has a local disconnect at the cabinet. Correct procedure is:

choices.text: Pull the disconnect and work — the switch is right there
Open the disconnect, apply your lock and tag, and verify dead with live-dead-live at the motor leads
Have the office turn off the breaker remotely
Work it live since fan circuits are low-amp

explanation: A local disconnect makes LOTO convenient, not optional. Lock, tag, and live-dead-live verification protect you on a roof exactly as anywhere — three-phase equipment demands it.

questionText: A system shows a 27°F supply-return split (rated ~18°F) and suction pressure is low. Before any refrigerant work you should:

choices.text: Check filter, blower wheel, coil, and duct restrictions — the high split points at low airflow

explanation: High split + low suction is the airflow signature: the same cooling packed into less air. Charging an airflow problem overcharges the system once airflow is restored — air before refrigerant, always.

questionText: A homeowner with a brand-new oversized AC reports the house is cold at 72°F but feels sticky and clammy. The mechanism is:

choices.text: Short cycling satisfies the thermostat before the coil runs long enough to remove moisture

explanation: Latent (moisture) removal requires runtime. The oversized unit wins the temperature race and loses the humidity war — the classic case against oversizing.

questionText: On a 95°F afternoon a condenser hums briefly then clicks off; the indoor blower runs fine. The highest-probability first test is:

choices.text: The dual run capacitor, measured against its rating after safe discharge

explanation: The hum-and-quit outdoor unit is the dual capacitor's signature — the single most common failure in residential cooling and the first meter test on any dead condenser.

questionText: A customer calls in a panic: their heat pump is 'on fire' with clouds pouring off it in 30°F weather. The unit resumes normal heating minutes later. You explain:

choices.text: That was a normal defrost cycle — hot gas melts coil frost and the meltwater flashes to visible steam

explanation: Defrost reverses hot gas through the frosted outdoor coil; melting frost produces dramatic but harmless steam clouds. Winter's most common false-alarm service call.

questionText: A furnace inducer runs but ignition never begins; the board flags the pressure switch. Your manometer at the switch tap reads well short of the switch's rating, and the intake/vent terminations are clear. The next suspect is:

choices.text: Inducer performance (weak motor, blocked wheel) or a blocked/cracked internal passage or hose — the draft is genuinely inadequate

explanation: The manometer proves the switch is telling the truth: draft is short. With terminations clear, the inducer, its wheel, hoses, and internal passages are the suspects — never jumper the switch.

questionText: During a fall PM, burner flames visibly disturb when the blower starts, and your analyzer shows CO rising in the supply air. Beyond documentation, the immediate professional action is:

choices.text: Treat as a breached heat exchanger: photograph/record the evidence, remove the furnace from service per procedure, and notify the customer in writing

explanation: Blower-coupled flame disturbance plus CO migration into supply air is condemnation-grade evidence of a breach — a CO hazard to the occupants. Document, red-tag per your authority, written notice.

questionText: With cooling called: 26VAC from R to C at the board, 26VAC from Y to C at the air handler, 0V from Y to C at the condenser. The fault is located:

choices.text: In the Y conductor or its splices between the air handler and the condenser

explanation: The call exists at the source and is missing at the destination: the wire path between them — classically chafed where it enters the condenser — is the fault.

questionText: A furnace board's 3A fuse blows every time the thermostat calls cooling, but heat works fine. The disciplined next step is:

choices.text: Hunt the short on the Y circuit — chafed low-voltage wire at the condenser or a shorted contactor coil

explanation: The fault appears only when Y energizes, which localizes the short to that circuit. The fuse is protecting the transformer; upsizing it just relocates the burning.

questionText: A two-stage, two-circuit RTU 'can't keep up' every afternoon. Circuit 1 pressures are normal; circuit 2's contactor never closes though the controls call Y2. Half of such complaints resolve at:

choices.text: The second stage: its control signal, contactor, or protective lockout — one circuit is doing the whole job

explanation: Staged RTUs degrade politely: one dead stage halves capacity while the unit 'runs fine.' Verify each circuit and each commanded stage independently.

questionText: You find an RTU economizer's dampers screwed shut by a previous tech. The office below reports afternoon stuffiness. Restoring the dampers matters because minimum position provides:

choices.text: The building's code-required outdoor ventilation air during occupied hours

explanation: Economizer dampers do double duty: free cooling when conditions allow, and the code minimum outdoor air always. Screwing them shut suffocates the occupants to silence a complaint.

questionText: After a building's electrical service work, an RTU scroll compressor runs loudly and produces no pressure differential. The check that explains it is:

choices.text: Phase rotation — a reversed sequence spins scroll compressors backwards

explanation: Scrolls only pump one direction. Reversed phase sequence after utility/service work is the classic cause: verify rotation, swap two legs at the disconnect, and recheck.

questionText: A boiler's relief valve discharges a little water every heating cycle while loop pressure swings from 15 to 29 psi during heat-up. The repair is:

choices.text: Replace or recharge the waterlogged expansion tank

explanation: Thermal expansion with nowhere to go becomes overpressure the relief must vent. The expansion tank absorbs it when healthy — and the relief valve is never, ever plugged.

questionText: On a chiller call you notice the cooling tower basin is green with growth and the water treatment feeder is empty, tagged out since spring. Beyond the chiller work, you must:

choices.text: Nothing — towers are the water company's problem
Document and report the untreated tower in writing — Legionella risk makes this a life-safety condition

explanation: Warm, aerated, untreated tower water is Legionella habitat, and drift spreads it. Like duct grease in kitchens: document, report in writing, and route to the treatment provider.

questionText: A restaurant's dining room doors whistle inward, candles gutter toward the kitchen, and the water heater's draft hood shows spillage. The kitchen exhaust runs constantly with a dead make-up air unit. The building condition is:

choices.text: Negative pressure: exhaust exceeds supply, the envelope is the make-up path, and combustion appliances are backdrafting — a CO hazard

explanation: The exhaust/supply ledger is unbalanced. Backdrafting flues make this a CO emergency-priority repair, not a comfort item — restore make-up air.

questionText: An AHU has run oddly for months. On the BAS you find its supply fan speed point in 'hand' at 45%, overridden during a service call last winter. The lesson generalized:

choices.text: Hand mode is a valid permanent setting
Check for forgotten overrides early on any BAS-served equipment — a point in hand ignores all logic and imitates every fault

explanation: Overrides outlive their reasons. The override scan is the BAS equivalent of checking the breaker — before hours of ghost-chasing.

questionText: A VFD-driven supply fan screams at 100%; the BAS shows duct static at 0.1 in. w.c. against a 1.5 setpoint. Your manometer at the sensor location reads 1.7. The repair is:

choices.text: Replace/recalibrate the duct static pressure sensor — the drive is faithfully chasing a lying reference
Slow the fan at the keypad and leave

explanation: Drives obey their reference. A dead-low static sensor makes the loop floor the fan; the manometer-vs-sensor comparison is the decisive test. Inputs before electronics.

questionText: Before insulation-testing (megger) a motor fed by a VFD, you must:

choices.text: Set the drive to zero speed
Disconnect the motor leads from the drive and verify the DC bus is discharged

explanation: Megger voltage into a connected drive destroys its output IGBTs, and the DC bus holds lethal charge after power-off — the UPS capacitor discipline verbatim.

questionText: Servicing a new R-454B system differs from R-410A practice because A2L refrigerants require:

choices.text: A2L-rated recovery equipment, elimination of ignition sources during work, and awareness of leak sensors and ventilation

explanation: Mildly flammable class: hard to ignite, slow flame, but the tooling ratings, spark discipline, and sensor/ventilation awareness are mandatory practice changes.

questionText: A running split system with a TXV needs its charge verified. Airflow has been confirmed. The correct verification is:

choices.text: Subcooling at the liquid line against the data-plate target
Frost line on the suction pipe

explanation: The TXV holds superheat steady regardless of charge, so subcooling carries the truth on TXV systems. Fixed-orifice systems use the superheat chart instead.

questionText: Months after a line-set repair done without flowing nitrogen, the system's TXV sticks and the new drier shows a temperature drop. The mechanism is:

choices.text: Cupric-oxide scale formed inside the tubing during open-air brazing, now harvested into the valve and drier

explanation: Brazing heat oxidizes the tube interior unless nitrogen displaces the air. The scale then circulates until the TXV screen and drier collect it — a five-cent purge prevents the cascade.

questionText: A customer asks for their 'usual spring pound of Freon.' The professional response is:

choices.text: Top it off — it's what they asked for
Explain that recurring loss means a leak: locate it, repair it, prove the repair, and document — topping off is not a service plan
Sell them two pounds to skip next spring

explanation: Annual top-offs rent the customer their own failure while venting regulated refrigerant by installment. Find, fix, prove, document.

questionText: Mid-summer no-cool: the indoor blower runs, the outdoor unit is silent, and the attic air handler's secondary pan is full with its float switch open. The correct service is:

choices.text: Bypass the float to restore cooling
Clear the primary drain, dry the pan, verify flow, and leave the float in service

explanation: The float interrupted cooling precisely to prevent a ceiling flood. The drain blockage is the repair; the protection stays.

questionText: After 'upgrading' to a dense MERV 13 filter in the original 1-inch slot, a system develops a frozen coil and a howling return. The engineering error was:

choices.text: Ignoring the static-pressure budget — high-MERV filtration needs more media area (deep-pleat cabinets), not a denser 1-inch filter

explanation: Filtration efficiency costs static pressure. Surface area buys it back; a dense 1-inch filter strangles airflow until the coil ices.

questionText: A single-phase compressor reads: C-R 1.8Ω, C-S 5.4Ω, R-S 7.2Ω, all terminals OL to shell. Start capacitor reads 4µF on an 88-108µF rating. The verdict is:

choices.text: Windings healthy (1.8+5.4=7.2, isolated from shell); replace the failed start capacitor

explanation: R-S equals C-R plus C-S with no shell continuity: the motor checks out. The collapsed start capacitor explains the no-start — the cheap part saves the expensive one.

questionText: During a 105°F record heat wave (design temp 95°F), a verified-healthy system runs continuously and holds 78°F against a 73°F setpoint. The honest customer conversation is:

choices.text: The system failed and needs replacement
The system is performing to its design: beyond design conditions, continuous running with some indoor drift is expected — options are load reduction or added capacity
It needs a pound of refrigerant

explanation: Equipment is sized to design conditions; extreme days exceed the contract. Honest explanation with options beats false condemnation.

questionText: A heat pump home is perfectly comfortable but January's electric bill tripled. The productive investigation is:

choices.text: Whether the compressor side has failed and the auxiliary strips are silently carrying the entire heating load

explanation: Strips maintain comfort at several times compressor-heat cost. 'Fine but expensive' is the classic silent-failure signature — verify what is actually producing the heat.

questionText: A conference room's CO2 logs 1,700 ppm during meetings but the demand-controlled ventilation damper never opens. Before touching the damper hardware, verify:

choices.text: The CO2 sensor's calibration — a drifted sensor never reports the occupancy that would open the damper

explanation: DCV obeys its sensor. Sensor truth before actuator hardware — the controls lesson that repeats across every module.

questionText: A spring PM finds a run capacitor at 37µF against a 45µF ±6% rating on a unit that 'runs fine.' The professional action is:

choices.text: Note it and recheck next year
Document the out-of-tolerance measurement and recommend replacement before the summer failure it predicts
Nothing — capacitors either work or they don't

explanation: Capacitors drift toward failure; catching a 17%-low reading in April prevents the July stranding. Measured, recorded PM is prediction, not inspection.

questionText: A new HVAC tech may legally do all of the following EXCEPT:

choices.text: Open a system's refrigerant circuit without EPA 608 certification

explanation: EPA Section 608 is the federal legal gate for opening refrigerant circuits and purchasing regulated refrigerants — the one hard line for the new tech.
referenceBookSection: Module 25 — Career in HVAC Field Service
